currentUse: St. George's Pro-Cathedral of the Independent Anglican Church, Canada Synod, at 1910 Falls St, Niagara Falls, NY 14303; also historically host to the Magdalene Project street-outreach ministry and its basement Rose Cafe
summary: The 1927 church building of St. George's (Šv. Jurgio), the only historically Lithuanian parish in Western New York, still stands at Falls and 19th Streets in Niagara Falls and is in use as a place of worship. The Roman Catholic parish was closed in the Diocese of Buffalo's "Journey in Faith and Grace" reorganization (sources give 2008 as its last year of operation as a debt-free parish; the Niagara Gazette dates the closure to 2009), with the Niagara Falls parishes merged into Divine Mercy Parish. In 2010 the Independent Anglican Church of Canada, led by Archbishop Peter Goodrich, purchased the church and rectory for $45,000, with proceeds directed to Divine Mercy Parish, and rededicated the building as St. George the Martyr on August 8, 2010; it became the synod's provisional episcopal seat (pro-cathedral) after the sale of its former cathedral in Cambridge, Ontario. The terms of the sale prevent Roman Catholic worship in the building. Lithuanian heritage markers survive: the neoclassical building is modeled after Vilnius Cathedral, and its stained glass windows depict St. George, St. Casimir and other saints with inscriptions in Lithuanian; the building is described as a designated historic landmark. The new owners based the Magdalene Project community outreach (Bibles, toiletries, food distribution) and a basement cafe for the homeless at the church. The Independent Anglican Church, Canada Synod's website continues to list St. George's Pro-Cathedral at 1910 Falls St, though no 2024-2026 press or dated service schedule for the Niagara Falls site was found.

developments.headline: Parish closed in Diocese of Buffalo reorganization
developments.detail: The Niagara Falls Historic Preservation Commission's history states the church 'remained open until 2008' as a debt-free, viable parish administered by the Pallottine Fathers; the Niagara Gazette dates the closure to 2009, part of a diocesan reorganization ('Journey in Faith and Grace') that closed eight Niagara Falls churches starting in 2006 and merged five parishes into Divine Mercy Parish.

sources.title: A rebirth for St. George's Church

developments.headline: Building sold to Independent Anglican Church of Canada for $45,000
developments.detail: The Independent Anglican Church of Canada, led by Archbishop Peter Goodrich, acquired the church and rectory at Falls and 19th Streets for $45,000, with proceeds directed to Divine Mercy Parish. The new owners announced worship services and the Magdalene Project outreach (Bibles, toiletries, food) with a basement cafe for vulnerable neighbors.

sources.title: A rebirth for St. George's Church

developments.headline: Rededicated as St. George the Martyr, provisional Anglican pro-cathedral
developments.detail: An Office of Dedication, Evensong, and Solemn Benediction was held August 8, 2010, with Archbishop Peter Goodrich officiating and Archdeacon/Vicar Michael William R. Stott preaching. The church became the provisional episcopal seat of the Independent Anglican Church, Canada Synod following the sale of its former cathedral in Cambridge, Ontario. The report notes the 1927 building is a designated historic landmark modeled after Vilnius Cathedral, with stained glass depicting St. George, St. Casimir and other saints bearing Lithuanian inscriptions, and that the terms of sale prevent Roman Catholic worship.

sources.title: Mystery Worshipper: St George the Martyr, Niagara Falls, New York, USA

developments.headline: Still listed as St. George's Pro-Cathedral by the Independent Anglican Church, Canada Synod
developments.detail: The denomination's website (undated, accessed July 2026) lists 'St. George's Pro-Cathedral, 1910 Falls St, Niagara Falls, NY, 14303' under its Diocese of St. George, with Peter W. Goodrich as Presiding Bishop. However, the site lists Sunday service times only for its two Hamilton, Ontario parishes, leaving the current level of activity at the Niagara Falls building unconfirmed.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: No contradictions — the found record confirms and refines the archive: the diocese (Buffalo) closed the parish and the building was sold to a non-Roman Anglican body (the Independent Anglican Church, Canada Synod) that uses it as a (pro-)cathedral, with Lithuanian heritage markers (Vilnius Cathedral-modeled architecture, Lithuanian-inscribed stained glass) preserved. Refinements: closure year is 2008 per the Niagara Falls Historic Preservation Commission ('remained open until 2008') vs. 2009 per the Niagara Gazette; sale was in 2010 for $45,000 with proceeds to the successor Divine Mercy Parish; the pro-cathedral status is 'provisional' episcopal seat. One anomaly: the Niagara Gazette article says the church was 'built in 1891 by Lithuanian immigrants,' which conflicts with the preservation commission's documented chronology (society founded 1902, first church 1915, present church 1927) and is likely a reporting error; the 1927 date is used here.
gaps: No press, diocesan, or community coverage from 2024-2026 was found confirming the current activity level of the Anglican congregation at 1910 Falls St — the denomination's undated website still lists the pro-cathedral, but its Sunday service schedule covers only its Hamilton, Ontario parishes, and church-directory entries mentioning a 9:30 Sunday mass, food pantry, and weekly lunches are undated aggregator data. One stale directory (alluschurches.com, surfaced in search results but consistent only with pre-2010 data) claims the building is 'for sale through Diocese of Buffalo,' contradicted by the documented 2010 sale. The exact canonical closure date (2008 vs 2009), the parish's founding year (sources give the 1902 benevolent society, 1915 first church; one search summary said the parish was established 1914 but I could not open a page confirming it), the specifics of the local landmark designation (Ship of Fools calls it 'a designated historic landmark'; a search summary attributed city landmark status but the designation record itself was not opened), and any Lithuanian-community reunions or preservation campaigns were not verifiable. The Am-Pol Eagle article 'St. George's continues as Anglican Pro-Cathedral' (ampoleagle.com) returned HTTP 403 and could not be read. Identity of the parish as the Lithuanian St. George's (not the Serbian/Antiochian Orthodox St. George congregations also in Niagara Falls) is confirmed by address and history.
